nativeRange.habitat: The Clown Knifefish inhabits large rivers, lakes, and floodplains across its native range, preferring sluggish or standing water with abundant cover such as submerged roots, overhanging vegetation, and flooded forest margins. It is largely nocturnal and tends to shelter in crevices or beneath debris during daylight hours.

decorPreferences: large driftwood and hollow logs for shelter
dim lighting or floating plants to diffuse light
large rounded substrate (sand or fine gravel)
minimal sharp decor (risk of injury to long delicate body)

diet.notes: The Clown Knifefish is an obligate piscivore in the wild, feeding on live fish, frogs, and large invertebrates. In captivity it can be trained onto meaty frozen foods such as large silversides, lance fish, prawns, and mussel, as well as high-protein pellets or sticks — though the process can take several weeks and generally requires offering food after lights-out when the fish is most active. Live feeder fish tend to accelerate initial food acceptance but carry a disease risk. Earthworms and market shrimp are often useful as transitional foods. Any tankmate small enough to fit in its mouth is at risk of being eaten.

goodWith: large predatory catfish (Sorubim, Pseudoplatystoma) of similar size
large Gourami species (Giant Gourami) if tank is large enough
large Tinfoil Barbs or similar robust fish too big to swallow
other large Notopterids only if tank is very large (500 L+ per fish) and juveniles introduced together
avoidWith: any fish small enough to be eaten (most community fish)
other knifefish in small tanks (severe conspecific aggression)
Black Ghost Knifefish (Apteronotus albifrons — unrelated family, different care needs, will be eaten or outcompeted)

imageCandidates.notes: Standard FishBase species image for Chitala ornata; shows adult lateral profile with characteristic white-ringed black spots on lower body.

summary: The Clown Knifefish is a striking freshwater predator from Southeast Asia that reaches up to 100 cm and requires at least 800 litres as an adult — far beyond most home setups. Sold as tiny juveniles, it is a nocturnal piscivore that will eat tankmates, and is considered unsuitable for the vast majority of home aquarists. Escaped specimens are now invasive in Florida.
careNotes: The Clown Knifefish (Chitala ornata) is one of Southeast Asia's largest freshwater fish and one of the most frequently impulse-purchased species that outgrows virtually every home aquarium. Juveniles are sold at 5–10 cm and can be visually captivating, but adults routinely reach 60–80 cm in captivity and can exceed 100 cm in the wild, requiring a tank of at least 800 L with a minimum 240 cm footprint — a setup beyond most hobbyists. This species is widely regarded as unsuitable for the majority of home aquarists due to its adult size alone. It is a dedicated nocturnal predator that will consume any tankmate small enough to fit in its mouth, including other knifefish kept in smaller tanks. Providing multiple large hiding places — driftwood, hollow logs, or large PVC pipes — is important for reducing stress, and dim lighting or floating-plant cover is strongly recommended, as the species tends to be photosensitive. Water quality must be kept pristine: the fish produces a high bioload and is sensitive to ammonia spikes, making a large and powerful filtration system essential. Chitala ornata is easily confused with the Royal Clown Knifefish (Chitala blanci), which bears dark vertical stripes instead of spots, and with the completely unrelated Black Ghost Knifefish (Apteronotus albifrons, family Apteronotidae) from South America. Escaped specimens have established invasive populations in Florida (USA), where the warm climate allows year-round survival; anyone who can no longer keep the fish must never release it into the wild.
breedingNotes: The Clown Knifefish is rarely bred in captivity. In the wild, pairs spawn on submerged flat surfaces or broad leaves, after which the male guards the egg mass and fans it with his anal fin until the eggs hatch. Captive spawning has been documented in very large public aquaria and specialized facilities using pairs housed in tanks exceeding 2,000 L. Hormonal induction has been used in Thailand for aquaculture purposes. Breeding is not considered achievable in home aquaria.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Chitala ornata
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 100 cm TL), temperature range (24–30°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness, native range (Chao Phraya and Mekong basins), habitat, diet (piscivore), conspecific behaviour, tank requirements, and breeding notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Chitala ornata
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Notopteridae, Osteoglossiformes), adult size (reported to 100 cm TL), native range (Indochina: Thailand, Cambodia, Laos, Vietnam, Myanmar), and diet classification (carnivore / piscivore). Also confirms introduction records in Florida, USA.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Clown Knifefish
additional.notes: Trade-focused care guide. Notable for explicit 'fish unsuitable for hobby' / extreme-size warning (reaches 3 ft / ~90 cm), minimum 200-gallon (757 L) tank recommendation, nocturnal piscivore feeding notes, and invasive species warning for Florida. Confirms tankmate concerns and difficulty training onto prepared foods.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Clown Knifefish profile
additional.notes: UK trade source; confirmed temperature 24–28°C, pH 6.5–7.5, minimum tank size (very large, 800 L+ implied), care level (expert), and noted species confusion with C. blanci (stripes vs spots) and Apteronotus albifrons (different family, South America).

additional.name: Florida Fish and Wildlife Conservation Commission — Clown Knifefish (invasive species)
additional.notes: Documents established feral populations in Florida originating from aquarium releases. Confirms reproduction in South Florida waterways. Used to populate invasive species context in careNotes.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding is extremely rare and essentially not achievable in home aquaria, requiring very large specialist facilities. In the wild, spawning occurs on flat surfaces with the male guarding the egg mass; hormonal induction has been used in Thai aquaculture to produce fry commercially.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches 80–100 cm; predatory and established invasive in Florida via aquarium releases. Hobby-unsuitable at adult size.
